title: Understanding Gender in Nouns and Body Parts with Examples from the Qur'an
summary: The session focused on explaining the gender of nouns in Modern Standard Arabic, particularly using examples from the Qur'an. The teacher discussed why certain words like 'sabeel' (path) are feminine despite lacking grammatical markers, attributing this to classical Arabic usage rather than specific rules. Key points included the importance of memorizing such exceptions and applying grammatical constructs like 'lam al-jar'. The lesson also covered body parts, noting that those with two counterparts (e.g., hands) are female while singular ones (e.g., nose) are male.
key_points: Gender of nouns in Arabic is sometimes based on classical usage rather than grammatical rules. Words like سَبِيل (sabeel, path) are feminine without the usual markers (تاء التбоة).
lam ال Lamb is a harf al-jar causing kasrah and indicating possession: هذا المكتب لسليم (This desk belongs to Salim).
Body parts with two counterparts (e.g., يد yad, hand) are feminine; singular parts (e.g., أنف anf, nose) are masculine.
Jiddan جِدًّا emphasizes adjectives: دَرّاجة لذيذة جِدًّا (a very tasty bicycle).
Using 'hath' versus 'hiya': حديث hadath is used for neutral objects, while Hiya refers to previously mentioned female entities.
questions: [00:01:00] Q: Why did Allah use the feminine 'hadhi' with Sabeel? — A: It's familiar due to the Prophet exemplifying it; Sabeel signifies an easy, accessible path. (Arabic terms: هذِه سَبِيلِي)
[00:03:00] Q: Why are some words female without markers? — A: They're 'Sama'i' (learned through hearing), e.g., Sabeel is feminine by classical use. (Arabic terms: سامي'
[00:12:02] Q: How to translate gender-neutral 'this'? — A: Use 'hadhi' for objects, not 'hiya', which refers to previously mentioned females.

quiz.q: Why is the word سَبِيل (sabīl, path) in the Qur'an sometimes referred to with the feminine demonstrative هَٰذِهِ (hādhihi)?
options: Because all paths are grammatically masculine in Arabic
Because it is a Samāʿī (heard usage) feminine noun in Arabic usage
Because it has a تاء التأنيث (feminine marker)
Because it refers only to women
quiz.a: The lecture explains that words like سَبِيل are feminine by سماعي (usage heard from Arabs), not because of a visible grammatical marker.

quiz.q: What does the letter ل (lām) as a حَرْفُ جَرّ (preposition) indicate in sentences like هٰذَا الْمَكْتَبُ لِسَلِيمٍ?
quiz.a: The lecture emphasized that لِ indicates possession, meaning 'belongs to', as in لِسَلِيمٍ (for Salim).

quiz.q: According to the lesson, how is the word دَرَّاجَة (darrājah, bicycle) described when emphasized with جِدًّا (jiddan)?
quiz.a: Adjectives must match the gender of the noun, so it becomes جَمِيلَةٌ (feminine) with جِدًّا for emphasis.

quiz.q: Which rule about body parts was taught in the lecture?
options: All body parts in Arabic are masculine
All body parts in Arabic are feminine
Body parts with two counterparts are feminine, singular ones are masculine
Body parts follow the gender of the person who owns them
quiz.a: The teacher explained that paired body parts like يَد (hand) are feminine, while singular ones like أَنْف (nose) are masculine.

quiz.q: What does the term سَمَاعِيّ (samāʿī) mean in Arabic grammar as explained in the lecture?
options: A rule based on hearing and usage from Arabs
A rule based on logic only
A rule used only in poetry
quiz.a: Samāʿī refers to forms learned through hearing native Arabic usage rather than deriving them from rules.

quiz.q: What is the meaning of the phrase هٰذَا الْمَكْتَبُ لِسَلِيمٍ?
options: This is a school for Salim
quiz.a: The lām (لِ) indicates possession, so it means 'this desk belongs to Salim'.

quiz.q: What is the function of جِدًّا (jiddan) in Arabic?
quiz.a: The lecture explained that جِدًّا is used to intensify adjectives, meaning 'very'.

quiz.q: What grammatical concept was repeatedly practiced with sentences like هٰذَا بَابٌ and هٰذِهِ سَيَّارَةٌ?
quiz.a: The lesson focused heavily on demonstratives هٰذَا (masculine) and هٰذِهِ (feminine).

quran_examples.arabic: قُلْ هَٰذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُو إِلَى ٱللَّهِ عَلَىٰ بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا۠ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِي
quran_examples.transliteration: Qul hādhihi sabīlī adʿū ilā Allāhi ʿalā baṣīrah anā wa mani ittabaʿanī
quran_examples.translation: Say, 'This is my way; I invite to Allah with insight, I and those who follow me.'
quran_examples.note: Illustrates the feminine demonstrative هَٰذِهِ (hādhihi) used with سَبِيل (sabīl), as discussed in the lecture.

quran_examples.translation: All praise is for Allah, Lord of the worlds.
quran_examples.note: Demonstrates the lām (لِ) of possession in لِلَّهِ, as explained in the lecture under حروف الجر.

quran_examples.translation: And the flesh of birds, from whatever they desire.
quran_examples.note: Used in the lecture to illustrate vocabulary like لَحْم (meat) and how nouns appear in Qur'anic descriptions.

quran_examples.translation: And Allah is swift in account.
quran_examples.note: Supports the vocabulary point of سَرِيع (quick) and its usage in Qur'anic descriptions.

quran_examples.translation: This is a clear statement for mankind.
quran_examples.note: Illustrates the demonstrative هَٰذَا (hādhā) used with masculine nouns, matching the lesson on demonstratives.
